text: Here you can see the left pulmonary artery is taking origin from the superior aspect of the right pulmonary artery.
To do this repair, we cannulated arterially in the distal ascending aorta and the right atrium for venous drainage.
Prior to dividing the left pulmonary artery, we attained vascular control of the proximal and distal aspects of the right pulmonary artery with tourniquets.
We also controlled the distal left pulmonary artery with a vascular clamp.
The left pulmonary artery is then divided from the right pulmonary artery.
It is important to note the left pulmonary artery is divided high to leave redundant tissue on the right pulmonary artery to prevent stenosis.
Additionally, the left pulmonary artery will need to be trimmed in order to avoid left pulmonary artery tortuosity.
Closing the defect in the right pulmonary artery is done by using the redundant tissue of the left pulmonary artery takeoff in two separate layers.
The free end of the left pulmonary artery sling needs to be retrieved and dissected out from the path between the trachea and the esophagus.
Once the free end of the left pulmonary artery is prepared, it is important to attain the final necessary length.
This is facilitated by filling the heart while on bypass to allow distention of the main pulmonary artery.
We have found the site of the ligamentum arteriosum provides a good implantation site if all ductal tissue is resected.
After the free end is trimmed to appropriate length, the heart can be decompressed on bypass and the anastomose can be performed.
For anastomosis, the left pulmonary artery is spatulated with a longer anterior hood and an appropriate arteriotomy is made in the middle of the main pulmonary artery, centered around the ligamentum.
To facilitate visualization, the main pulmonary artery is controlled with a vascular clamp throughout the anastomosis.
This is the final result of the reimplantation.
It is worth noting the right pulmonary artery has not been narrowed throughout the procedure.
